current_fact.fact_text: On May 10, 1946, the United States successfully launched a captured German V‑2 rocket from White Sands Proving Ground in New Mexico, the first fully successful American test of the technology. These flights marked the dawn of the U.S. rocket program and paved the way—from wartime missiles—to peaceful space exploration.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: A captured missile becoming the starting point for spaceflight parallels an episode titled "Destination Sedna," a sonic voyage outward that echoes humanity’s first serious steps toward the cosmos.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1961, Air France Flight 406 was destroyed by a bomb over the Sahara Desert, killing all 78 people on board. The mid‑air explosion scattered wreckage over remote dunes, highlighting how vast, seemingly empty landscapes can become sudden stages for human tragedy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A bombing over the Sahara connects directly with an episode rooted in desert soundscapes and Tuareg experience, bringing human stories and resilience into that same sand‑shaped region.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1967, the experimental Northrop M2‑F2 lifting‑body aircraft crashed on landing at Edwards Air Force Base, tumbling violently across the dry lakebed. Footage of the crash later inspired the opening credits of the TV series "The Six Million Dollar Man," turning a real test‑pilot accident into a pop‑culture story of a rebuilt cyborg hero.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A real pilot nearly destroyed and then fictionalized as a rebuilt cyborg hero mirrors the theme of transformation and alternate identities explored through musical alter‑egos in this episode.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1997, a magnitude 7.3 earthquake struck Iran’s Khorasan Province near the city of Qayen, killing over 1,500 people and destroying numerous villages. The disaster exposed the vulnerability of traditional mud‑brick housing to seismic waves and spurred renewed focus on earthquake‑resistant construction in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden seismic jolt reshaping landscapes connects metaphorically to an "Electric Shock" of guitar‑driven energy, amplifying the raw, disruptive force of tectonic movement.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 2013, One World Trade Center in New York City reached its full height of 1,776 feet when the final spire section was installed, making it the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere. Designed with layered symbolism—from its height to its prism‑like glass—this skyscraper rose as a highly visible statement of memory, resilience, and urban reinvention.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

queue.match_reason: A towering symbol in New York’s skyline suits an urban, city‑walking episode from London; both reflect how modern cities turn architecture, streets, and sound into living postcards of resilience.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 2013, a bomb hidden on Air France Flight 406—sorry, different event already used; instead, note that on this date the Western Hemisphere’s tallest building rose while elsewhere in history different towers fell. One World Trade Center’s spire completed a vertical arc from tragedy to reconstruction, turning absence into a new, reflective presence in the sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: The emotional movement from loss to rebuilding embodied by One World Trade Center aligns with an episode about transitions, storms passing, and the decision to keep moving forward with hope.

queue.fact_text: Looking back again to May 10, 1933, the same Nazi book burnings that darkened German university squares also sparked a counter‑movement abroad: in many countries, librarians and writers began consciously preserving banned works. The flames in one place unexpectedly seeded archives and reading circles in another, turning censorship into a strange catalyst for literary survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: A regime trying to control what people read contrasts with an episode about choosing what we hear with care; both wrestle with who gets to decide which voices survive in our daily lives.

queue.fact_text: Again on May 10, 1503, Columbus’s naming of "Las Tortugas" for the Cayman Islands shows how first impressions can freeze complex ecosystems into a single image. Behind that simple turtle‑filled snapshot lies a rich web of coral reefs, sharks, and migratory paths that only centuries of biology could begin to map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Revisiting an island named for turtles, we emphasize the hidden complexity of marine life, resonating with an episode that dives deep beneath the surface to meet orcas and whales in their own element.

queue.fact_text: Finally, on May 10, 2024, as auroras danced far from their usual polar homes, scientists rushed to calibrate models and operators put satellites into safe mode, reminding us that the Sun is not a distant backdrop but a changing, sometimes volatile neighbor. Each geomagnetic storm is both a hazard and a natural experiment, letting us probe the invisible magnetic architecture that shields our planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A geomagnetic storm rewriting earthly skies suits the boundary‑blurring atmospheres of trip‑hop, where invisible forces and low‑frequency pulses subtly reshape the emotional weather of a night.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 2024, a series of powerful geomagnetic storms began, triggered by multiple coronal mass ejections from the Sun. These "May 2024 solar storms" were the strongest since the 2003 Halloween storms and caused auroras to be seen unusually far toward the equator, along with minor disruptions to satellites and power infrastructure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A stormy solar event that literally reshaped near-Earth space pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, underscored by bright, propulsive electronic textures.

published.fact_text: In 28 BC, astronomers of China’s Han dynasty recorded a dark mark crossing the Sun—one of the earliest reliably dated observations of a sunspot. Long before telescopes, they used careful naked-eye observations at sunrise and sunset to track solar blemishes and link them to omens and natural changes.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: Ancient skywatchers quietly tracking blemishes on the Sun fits an open, drifting "Evening Flow" introduction that invites slow, attentive looking and listening beyond the obvious.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1916, Ernest Shackleton completed an 800‑nautical‑mile voyage in a tiny lifeboat, the James Caird, landing on South Georgia after navigating some of the roughest seas on Earth. The improbable journey, guided by dead reckoning and a sextant in sub‑Antarctic storms, became one of history’s most celebrated feats of survival and navigation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A legendary open‑boat crossing through hostile seas resonates with an episode literally recorded on a sailing boat, celebrating resilience, companionship, and life on the water.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1996, a sudden blizzard struck Mount Everest, trapping multiple climbing teams high on the mountain and leading to the deaths of eight climbers by the next day. The tragedy, later chronicled in memoirs and films, forced the mountaineering world to rethink the commercialization of Everest and the razor‑thin margin for error in extreme-altitude climbing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A deadly Himalayan storm and an Arctic orca expedition share cold, remote extremes and the thin line between awe and danger, reflected in the episode’s cinematic, winter‑lit mood.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1869, the First Transcontinental Railroad in the United States was completed at Promontory Summit, Utah Territory, when a ceremonial golden spike was driven to join the eastern and western lines. In just six years, rail had shrunk a months‑long overland journey into about a week, rewiring trade, migration, and the very sense of distance across a continent.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A railroad that collapsed distances and set a continent "on the move" aligns with a high‑energy travel‑themed mix built for motion, fresh starts, and long journeys.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1975, Sony introduced the Betamax videocassette recorder, bringing high‑quality home video recording to consumers for the first time. Although Betamax would ultimately lose the famous "format war" to VHS, it helped launch the entire era of time‑shifting TV, home taping, and a new kind of media control by ordinary viewers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: A pioneering home‑video format that changed how we consume media pairs with an episode about early‑2000s MP3 nostalgia, both tracing how technology reshaped our private entertainment rituals.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1922, the United States formally annexed Kingman Reef, a remote, largely submerged coral atoll in the central Pacific Ocean. Though tiny and uninhabited, the reef later gained strategic and ecological importance as a navigational waypoint and as a protected marine area brimming with ocean life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A remote Pacific reef rich with underwater life fits an episode built around immersion, floating, and water‑like musical flow, echoing the quiet mystery of an ocean outpost.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1503, Christopher Columbus reached the Cayman Islands on his fourth voyage and named them "Las Tortugas" because of the abundance of sea turtles he encountered. The moment captures how early European explorers often used wildlife as their first language for naming and understanding new lands.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Columbus naming islands after turtles and a modern diver seeking orcas both tell of first encounters with marine megafauna, mirrored by the episode’s ocean‑swept, cinematic mood.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1497, Amerigo Vespucci is believed to have departed from Cádiz on a voyage that would contribute to the realization that the Americas were not Asia but a "New World." His accounts, though debated, helped inspire cartographers to eventually name two continents after him: America.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: Vespucci’s departure from a Mediterranean port toward unknown coasts aligns with a sun‑drenched episode about boat journeys and cross‑sea musical currents.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1775, a small force of American colonial militia led by Ethan Allen and Benedict Arnold captured Fort Ticonderoga from the British in a pre‑dawn raid. The lightly defended fort yielded badly needed artillery that would later be dragged across winter snows to help break the British siege of Boston.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A surprise seizure of a fortress at the dawn of the American Revolution matches the energy and political charge of an episode dedicated to Rage Against the Machine and acts of resistance.

published.fact_text: Also on May 10, 1775, delegates from the Thirteen Colonies convened in Philadelphia as the Second Continental Congress, gradually transforming from a loose meeting of colonial representatives into a de facto national government. Within weeks they would appoint George Washington to lead a continental army and eventually move toward declaring independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A fledgling congress learning to govern and redefine itself mirrors an episode about iterative learning, mistakes, and growing into a new role over time.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1688, King Narai of the Ayutthaya Kingdom appointed Phetracha as regent, setting in motion the Siamese revolution of 1688. The move ultimately led to Narai’s death, the expulsion of many foreign influences, and Phetracha’s rise as king, dramatically reshaping Siam’s political and cultural orientation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A pivotal succession crisis in Siam directly connects to an episode recorded in modern Thailand, weaving historical power shifts into today’s cultural and musical landscape.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1796, Napoleon Bonaparte won the Battle of Lodi in northern Italy, driving Austrian forces back across the Adda River. Although not a large battle by casualties, Napoleon later mythologized it as the moment he felt destined for greatness, using the story to forge his own legend.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An iconic Napoleonic victory on Italian soil fits an episode explicitly exploring contrasting Italian energies, from northern fog to southern fire.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1933, Nazi organizations in Germany orchestrated public book burnings, hurling thousands of works by Jewish, leftist, and "un-German" authors into bonfires. The ritualized destruction was a powerful symbol of cultural repression, aiming to erase ideas and voices considered threatening to the regime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A regime trying to literally burn away ideas contrasts with an episode about refusing labels and asserting complex identities, carried by introspective, resistant hip‑hop.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1940, during the early months of World War II, German bombers accidentally attacked the German city of Freiburg im Breisgau, killing civilians in a friendly‑fire raid that was initially blamed on the Allies. The incident shows how the fog of war can obscure responsibility and truth, even for those launching the bombs.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: An accidental bombing that shattered its own city pairs, in title and metaphor, with "November Bomb"—a musical blast that marks a beginning rather than a tragedy, reclaiming the word with creative energy.

These flights marked the dawn of the U.S. rocket program and paved the way—from wartime missiles—to peaceful space exploration.
